questions.title: Medication Administration
questions.text: A nurse is preparing to administer medications to a client. To ensure client safety, which action should the nurse perform FIRST?

choices.text: Document the medication administration.
Check the client's identification using two identifiers.
Explain the purpose of the medication to the client.
Check drug incompatibilities.

questions.rationale: Ensuring the 'Right Patient' is a crucial first step in the medication administration process according to the 'Rights of Medication Administration'. This is accomplished by using at least two client identifiers (e.g., name and date of birth, name and medical record number) and comparing them against the medication administration record (MAR). While explaining the medication (C) and performing checks (D) are vital, verifying the patient's identity (B) must occur before proceeding. Documentation (A) occurs after administration.

questions.title: Vital Signs Assessment
questions.text: A nurse is assessing the vital signs of a client. Which assessment finding requires immediate intervention?

choices.text: Temperature of 99.1°F (37.3°C)
Respiratory rate of 8 breaths per minute
Blood pressure of 110/70 mmHg
Heart rate of 88 beats per minute

questions.rationale: A respiratory rate of 8 breaths per minute indicates hypoventilation (normal adult range is 12-20 breaths per minute) and requires immediate intervention to prevent respiratory arrest and ensure adequate oxygenation. The temperature of 99.1°F is slightly elevated but not significantly abnormal. Blood pressure of 110/70 mmHg is within normal range. Heart rate of 88 beats per minute is also within normal range (60-100 beats per minute).

questions.title: Fall Prevention
questions.text: A nurse is caring for an elderly client who has been identified as high risk for falls. Which intervention is most important to implement?

choices.text: Maintain bed in lowest position when the client is unattended
Administer sleep medication at bedtime
Encourage the client to remain in bed
Apply restraints when the client is agitated

questions.rationale: Maintaining the bed in the lowest position when the client is unattended reduces the risk of injury if a fall occurs. This is an important environmental safety measure in fall prevention. Administering sleep medication can increase fall risk due to sedation and is not recommended as a fall prevention strategy. Restricting mobility by encouraging bed rest can lead to deconditioning and paradoxically increase fall risk. Restraints are not recommended as they can cause physical and psychological harm, may increase agitation, and are subject to strict regulatory requirements.

questions.title: Oxygen Administration
questions.text: A nurse is caring for a client receiving oxygen at 2 L/min via nasal cannula. Which assessment finding would indicate that the oxygen therapy is effective?

choices.text: Oxygen saturation of 88%
Respiratory rate of 28 breaths per minute
Absence of cyanosis with oxygen saturation of 94%
Use of accessory muscles during respiration

questions.rationale: The absence of cyanosis with an oxygen saturation of 94% indicates effective oxygen therapy, as it shows adequate oxygenation of tissues (normal saturation is 95-100%, but 94% may be acceptable depending on the client's condition). An oxygen saturation of 88% indicates hypoxemia and is below the target range for most patients. A respiratory rate of 28 breaths per minute indicates tachypnea, suggesting respiratory distress. Use of accessory muscles indicates increased work of breathing and respiratory distress, suggesting inadequate oxygenation.

questions.title: Sterile Technique
questions.text: A nurse is performing a sterile dressing change. Which action would break sterile technique?

choices.text: Opening sterile packages away from the face
Keeping sterile items above waist level
Reaching over the sterile field to retrieve an item
Using sterile gloves to handle sterile supplies

questions.rationale: Reaching over a sterile field breaks sterile technique because the sleeve and arm passing over the field are not sterile and can contaminate the field below. Opening sterile packages away from the face prevents contamination from respiratory droplets. Keeping sterile items above waist level maintains sterility as items below waist level are considered contaminated. Using sterile gloves to handle sterile supplies maintains sterile technique.

questions.title: Pain Assessment
questions.text: A nurse is assessing a client's pain. Which assessment finding is the most reliable indicator of pain?

choices.text: Elevated blood pressure
Client's self-report of pain
Facial grimacing
Increased heart rate

questions.rationale: The client's self-report of pain is considered the most reliable indicator of pain, as pain is a subjective experience that only the person experiencing it can truly describe. While physiological signs like elevated blood pressure and increased heart rate can be associated with pain, they are not specific to pain and can be caused by various factors. Behavioral cues like facial grimacing can indicate pain but are less reliable than self-reporting, especially in clients who can communicate.

questions.title: Enteral Nutrition
questions.text: A nurse is caring for a client receiving continuous enteral feeding via a nasogastric tube. Which nursing action is most important in preventing aspiration?

choices.text: Checking tube placement every 4 hours
Maintaining head of bed elevation at 30-45 degrees
Administering the feeding at room temperature
Flushing the tube with water every 8 hours

questions.rationale: Maintaining the head of bed elevation at 30-45 degrees during continuous enteral feeding reduces the risk of aspiration by using gravity to help prevent reflux of gastric contents. Checking tube placement is important but is primarily to ensure proper delivery of nutrition rather than aspiration prevention. The temperature of the feeding does not significantly affect aspiration risk. Flushing the tube is important to maintain patency but is not the most important intervention for preventing aspiration.

questions.title: Wound Care
questions.text: A nurse is assessing a client's pressure ulcer and notes that the wound extends through the dermis into the subcutaneous tissue with visible yellowish slough. According to the National Pressure Ulcer Advisory Panel classification, what stage is this pressure ulcer?

choices.text: Stage 1
Stage 2
Stage 3
Unstageable

questions.rationale: A Stage 3 pressure ulcer extends through the dermis into the subcutaneous tissue without exposing bone, tendon, or muscle. The presence of yellowish slough does not make it unstageable unless the slough completely obscures the wound bed. Stage 1 is intact skin with non-blanchable redness. Stage 2 involves partial-thickness skin loss with exposed dermis. Unstageable pressure ulcers have full-thickness tissue loss in which the base of the ulcer is completely covered by slough or eschar, preventing direct visualization of the wound bed.

questions.title: Urinary Catheter Care
questions.text: A nurse is providing care for a client with an indwelling urinary catheter. Which action would help prevent catheter-associated urinary tract infection (CAUTI)?

choices.text: Irrigating the catheter regularly with normal saline
Securing the catheter to the thigh to prevent movement
Positioning the drainage bag above the level of the bladder
Changing the catheter and drainage system weekly

questions.rationale: Securing the catheter to the thigh prevents movement and traction on the urethra, which can reduce urethral irritation and the risk of microorganisms ascending into the bladder. Routine irrigation is not recommended as it can introduce bacteria. The drainage bag should be positioned below, not above, the level of the bladder to prevent backflow of urine. Routine catheter changes are not recommended; catheters should only be changed based on clinical indications such as obstruction or infection.

questions.title: Therapeutic Communication
questions.text: A client says to the nurse, "No one cares about me. I might as well die." Which response by the nurse demonstrates therapeutic communication?

choices.text: "Don't say that. Many people care about you."
"I understand how you feel. I've felt that way too."
"Are you having thoughts of harming yourself?"
"Let's talk about something more positive."

questions.rationale: Asking "Are you having thoughts of harming yourself?" directly addresses the client's statement about wanting to die and assesses suicide risk, which is a priority when a client makes such statements. Saying "Don't say that" invalidates the client's feelings. Stating "I understand how you feel" when the nurse may not truly understand can be perceived as insincere. Changing the subject to something more positive avoids addressing the client's concerning statement and misses an opportunity to assess suicide risk.

questions.title: Prioritization of Care
questions.text: A nurse is caring for four clients. Which client should the nurse assess first?

choices.text: A client who had surgery yesterday and is due for pain medication
A client with diabetes who reports feeling dizzy and sweaty
A client with pneumonia who needs to be repositioned
A client who is requesting assistance to the bathroom

questions.rationale: The client with diabetes reporting dizziness and sweating should be assessed first as these symptoms suggest hypoglycemia, which can rapidly progress to loss of consciousness and is potentially life-threatening if not treated promptly. While pain management is important, it is not immediately life-threatening. Repositioning a client with pneumonia is important for prevention of complications but is not as urgent as potential hypoglycemia. Assisting a client to the bathroom is important for comfort and dignity but is not as urgent as a potentially life-threatening situation.

questions.title: Documentation
questions.text: A client reports having a headache with a pain level of 7/10. The nurse administers the prescribed analgesic. Which documentation by the nurse is most appropriate?

choices.text: "Client states having headache. Medication given."
"Client reports headache rated 7/10. Acetaminophen 650 mg administered PO at 1400. Will reassess pain in 30 minutes."
"Headache present. Analgesic administered as ordered."
"Client uncomfortable due to headache. Appropriate intervention provided."

questions.rationale: "Client reports headache rated 7/10. Acetaminophen 650 mg administered PO at 1400. Will reassess pain in 30 minutes." is the most appropriate documentation as it includes specific details about the assessment (pain level), intervention (specific medication, dose, route, time), and plan for follow-up (reassessment time). The other options lack specific details about the medication administered, the pain assessment using a numeric scale, and/or the plan for follow-up, which are all essential components of proper nursing documentation.

questions.title: Infection Control
questions.text: A nurse enters the room of a client on contact precautions for methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) infection. Which action should the nurse take first?

choices.text: Put on a gown and gloves
Put on a mask
Perform hand hygiene
Educate the client about contact precautions

questions.rationale: Hand hygiene is the first step in preventing the transmission of infection in any setting, including before entering a room on contact precautions. After hand hygiene, the nurse would then don appropriate personal protective equipment (PPE), which for contact precautions typically includes a gown and gloves. A mask is not routinely required for contact precautions unless indicated for standard precautions or if the client has respiratory symptoms. Education is important but would not be performed before hand hygiene and donning appropriate PPE.

questions.title: Mobility and Transfer
questions.text: A nurse is preparing to transfer a client from the bed to a chair. Which action is most important for preventing injury to the nurse?

choices.text: Twisting at the waist while lifting the client
Keeping the client close to the nurse's body during transfer
Bending at the waist to lift the client
Lifting with the back muscles

questions.rationale: Keeping the client close to the nurse's body during transfer reduces the strain on the nurse's back and decreases the risk of injury by minimizing the lever arm effect. Twisting at the waist while lifting increases the risk of back injury and should be avoided. Bending at the waist to lift also increases strain on the back; instead, nurses should bend at the knees and hips, keeping the back straight. Lifting should be done using the stronger leg muscles rather than back muscles, which are more prone to injury.

questions.title: Ethics in Nursing
questions.text: A family member asks the nurse not to tell the client about a newly diagnosed terminal illness. How should the nurse respond?

choices.text: "I understand your concern, but I am obligated to provide information if the client asks me directly."
"I will respect your wishes and not discuss the diagnosis with the client."
"I suggest we discuss this with the healthcare team to develop a plan for disclosure."
"I will need to inform the client immediately, as they have a right to know."

questions.rationale: Suggesting a discussion with the healthcare team to develop a plan for disclosure acknowledges the family's concerns while recognizing the ethical principles involved. This approach respects the collaborative nature of healthcare decisions and the complexity of terminal illness disclosure. Simply agreeing not to tell the client violates the principle of autonomy and the client's right to information about their health. Immediately informing the client without consideration of timing, cultural factors, or the family's concerns may not be the most compassionate approach. While the nurse is obligated to provide truthful information if asked directly, this response doesn't address the need for a coordinated approach to disclosure.

questions.title: Intravenous Therapy
questions.text: A nurse is administering intravenous (IV) fluids to a client. The nurse observes redness, swelling, and pain at the IV insertion site. What is the priority nursing action?

choices.text: Apply warm compresses to the site
Increase the flow rate of the IV fluid
Discontinue the IV and restart at a different site
Administer the prescribed IV push medication

questions.rationale: The signs of redness, swelling, and pain at the IV insertion site indicate phlebitis or infiltration, necessitating immediate discontinuation of the IV and restarting at a different site to prevent further complications. Applying warm compresses may be appropriate after the IV is discontinued, but is not the priority action. Increasing the flow rate would worsen the condition by introducing more fluid into the tissues or irritating the vein further. Administering an IV push medication into a compromised IV site could cause significant tissue damage, especially if the medication is vesicant.
